source.answer_key_text: If a < x ≤ b, the mean-value theorem assures us that f(x) - f(a) = f'(t)(x - a) for some t in (a, x).

classification.course_scope: AP Calculus AB/BC
classification.crosswalk_note: Not mapped to the current AP Calculus AB/BC core in this first-pass crosswalk.
classification.framework: AP Calculus Course and Exam Description, current Unit 1 topic framework

questions.prompt_html: If f'(x) = 0 throughout an interval [a, b], prove that f(x) is constant on the interval.
questions.prompt_text: If f'(x) = 0 throughout an interval [a, b], prove that f(x) is constant on the interval.
questions.solution_html:
If a < x ≤ b, the mean-value theorem assures us that f(x) - f(a) = f'(t)(x - a) for some t in (a, x).
Since f'(t) = 0, we must have f(x) = f(a). This is true for all x in [a, b].
questions.solution_text: If a < x ≤ b, the mean-value theorem assures us that f(x) - f(a) = f'(t)(x - a) for some t in (a, x). Since f'(t) = 0, we must have f(x) = f(a). This is true for all x in [a, b].

feedback_html.solution:
If a < x ≤ b, the mean-value theorem assures us that f(x) - f(a) = f'(t)(x - a) for some t in (a, x).
Since f'(t) = 0, we must have f(x) = f(a). This is true for all x in [a, b].

questions.prompt_html:
The Mean-Value Theorem for integrals asserts that: If f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists a point c in [ a, b] such that  dx = (b - a) f(c).
Verify that the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied to the function f(x) =   
on the interval [a, b] = [0, 3], and find the point c guaranteed by the Theorem.
questions.prompt_text: The Mean-Value Theorem for integrals asserts that: If f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists a point c in [ a, b] such that integral of (f(x)) from (a) to (b) dx = (b - a) f(c). Verify that the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied to the function f(x) = (x/square root of (16 + (x) with superscript (2))) on the interval [a, b] = [0, 3], and find the point c guaranteed by the Theorem.
questions.solution_html:
First, observe that the function f(x) =  is continuous on the interval [0, 3]. Therefore the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied. Let us compute the left and right hand sides of the equation separately.
Right Hand Side: (b - a) f(c) = ( 3 - 0)  = 
Left Hand Side: I = dx = dx.
We shall use the substitution u = 16 + x2, hence du = 2x dx or x dx =  du.
Note also that at x = 0, u = 16, and at x = 3, u = 16 + 9 = 25. We obtain 
  I =  du =  =  -  = 5 - 4 = 1
Equating left and right hand sides, we obtain: 1 =  or  = 3c.
  To solve for c, let us square both sides to get 16 + c2 = 9c2 .  
It follows that 8c2 = 16, hence c = +  ( since c > 0 ).

Therefore the Mean-Value Theorem for integrals can be applied. Let us compute the left and right hand sides of the equation separately. Right Hand Side: (b - a) f(c) = ( 3 - 0) (c/square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2))) = (3c/square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2))) Left Hand Side: I = integral of (f(x)) from (a) to (b) dx = integral of ((x/square root of (16 + (x) with superscript (2)))) from (0) to (3) dx. We shall use the substitution u = 16 + x 2 , hence du = 2x dx or x dx = (1/2) du. Note also that at x = 0, u = 16, and at x = 3, u = 16 + 9 = 25. We obtain I = (1/2) integral of ((1/square root of (u))) from (16) to (25) du = (square root of (u)) superscript (u=25) subscript (u=16) = square root of (25) - square root of (16) = 5 - 4 = 1 Equating left and right hand sides, we obtain: 1 = (3c/square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2))) or square root of (16 + (c) with superscript (2)) = 3c. To solve for c, let us square both sides to get 16 + c 2 = 9c 2 . It follows that 8c 2 = 16, hence c = + square root of (2 ) ( since c > 0 ).

questions.prompt_html: Find the empirical regression line y = ax + b for the data (x, y) = (1, -2), (4, 1), and (1, 10). What is the predicted value of y when x = -3?
questions.prompt_text: Find the empirical regression line y = ax + b for the data (x, y) = (1, -2), (4, 1), and (1, 10). What is the predicted value of y when x = -3?
questions.solution_html:
a = -1 and b = 5; empirical regression line y = -x + 5 . 
The predicted value of y when x = - 3 is y = - (- 3) + 5 = 8

source.answer_key_text: A function V(x, y) is called a Liapunov function for an autonomous system associated with vector field F if

classification.course_scope: Beyond AP Calculus
classification.crosswalk_note: Not mapped to the current AP Calculus AB/BC core in this first-pass crosswalk.
classification.framework: AP Calculus Course and Exam Description, current Unit 1 topic framework

questions.prompt_html:
Define carefully what is meant by: 
The function V(x,y) is a Liapunov function in a domain D (containing the fixed point at the origin) for the autonomous system associated with the vector field F = P(x,y) i + Q(j, x,y).
Assume that P and Q have continuous partial derivatives in D.
questions.prompt_text: Define carefully what is meant by: The function V(x,y) is a Liapunov function in a domain D (containing the fixed point at the origin) for the autonomous system associated with the vector field F = P(x,y) i + Q(j, x,y). Assume that P and Q have continuous partial derivatives in D.
questions.solution_html:
A function V(x, y) is called a Liapunov function for an autonomous system associated with vector field F if
(i)V(x,y) has continuous partials in D
(ii)V(x,y) is positive definite in D [that is, V(x,y) > 0 for all (x,y) ≠ (0, 0) and V(x,y) = 0 
if and only if (x, y) = (0, 0)]
(iii)= (∇ V)∙ F ≤  0 in D (that is,  is negative semi definite in D)
questions.solution_text: A function V(x, y) is called a Liapunov function for an autonomous system associated with vector field F if (i) V(x,y) has continuous partials in D (ii) V(x,y) is positive definite in D [that is, V(x,y) > 0 for all (x,y) ≠ (0, 0) and V(x,y) = 0 if and only if (x, y) = (0, 0)] (iii) (dV/dt) = (∇ V)∙ F ≤ 0 in D (that is, (dV/dt) is negative semi definite in D)

feedback_html.solution:
A function V(x, y) is called a Liapunov function for an autonomous system associated with vector field F if
(i)V(x,y) has continuous partials in D
(ii)V(x,y) is positive definite in D [that is, V(x,y) > 0 for all (x,y) ≠ (0, 0) and V(x,y) = 0 
if and only if (x, y) = (0, 0)]
(iii)= (∇ V)∙ F ≤  0 in D (that is,  is negative semi definite in D)

Fit a line of the form ln(V) = m∙a + b to the data, where V is the resale value in thousands of dollars and a is the age of the car in years..

questions.prompt_html:
Write Converse and Contrapositive
Write the converse and contrapositive.
questions.prompt_text: 1) If it rains today, then it will snow tomorrow. A) Converse: If it snows tomorrow, then it will rain today. Contrapositive: If it does not snow tomorrow, then it will not rain today. B) Converse: If it snows tomorrow, then it will rain today. Contrapositive: If it does not rain today, then it will not snow tomorrow. C) Converse: If it does not snow tomorrow, then it will not rain today. Contrapositive: If it snows tomorrow, then it will rain today. D) Converse: If it snows tomorrow, then it will not rain today. Contrapositive: If it does not snow tomorrow, then it will rain today.

metadata.math_format: vector PDF crop plus searchable extracted text

questions.prompt_html:
Write Converse and Contrapositive
Write the converse and contrapositive.
questions.prompt_text: 2) If angle ABC is obtuse, then its measure is greater than 90° and less than 180° . A) Converse: If the measure of angle ABC is greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is obtuse. Contrapositive: If the measure of angle ABC is not greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is not obtuse. B) Converse: If the measure of angle ABC is not greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is not obtuse. Contrapositive: If the measure of angle ABC is greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is obtuse. C) Converse: If the measure of angle ABC is greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is obtuse. Contrapositive: If angle ABC is not obtuse, then its measure is not greater than 90° and less than 180° . D) Converse: If angle ABC is not obtuse, then its measure is not greater than 90° and less than 180° . Contrapositive: If the measure of angle ABC is not greater than 90° and less than 180°, then it is not obtuse.

Contrapositive: If a2 > b2 then a > b . C) Converse: If a2 ≯b2 then a > b . Contrapositive: If a2 > b2 then a≯ b. D) Converse: If a2 > b2 then a > b . Contrapositive: If a ≯ b then a2 ≥ b2.

Write a function C(x) that gives the cost of laying the cable in terms of the distance x. 1 mi 700 ft A) C(x) = 175 x2 + 7002 + 150(5280 - x) B) C(x) = 175 x2 + 7002 + 150(1 - x) C) C( ) 150 2 7002 175(5280 ) D) C( ) 175(700 ) 150(1 )

questions.prompt_text: 9) A glass-cage elevator rises on the outside of a hotel which is h m tall. A person stands 100 m from the base of the elevator. Express the distance d from the person to the elevator as a function of the height x of the elevator. What is the range of d = f(x)? A) d = 1002 + x2 : 100 ≤ d ≤ 1002 + h2 B) d = 1002 - x2 : 100 ≤ d ≤ 1002 - h2 C) d = 1002 + x2 : 0 ≤ d ≤ 100 D) d = 1002 + x2 : d ≤ 100

The function g(t) = 0.5t2 + 11.85t + 107.6 models the total yearly cost of Medicare in billions of dollars, where t represents years after 1990. What does the function g f represent? Find g f (10). A) Cost per person in thousands of dollars. $12.33 thousand B) Cost per person in thousands of dollars. $0.18 thousand C) Cost per person in thousands of dollars. $0.08 thousand D) Cost per person in thousands of dollars. $9.26 thousand

questions.prompt_text: 2) After being in business for t years, a manufacturer of radios is making 1500 + 400t + 30t2 units per year. A) R(t) = (1500 + 400t + 30t2)(30 + 3t) B) R(t) = (1500 + 400t + 30t2) + (30 + 3t) C) R(t) = (1500 + 400t + 30t2) - (30 + 3t) D) R(t) = (1500 + 400t + 30t2) (30 + 3t)
